proposal_text: This research proposes to deploy a real-time facial recognition system across Trinity College Dublin campus buildings for automated security screening. The system will use CCTV feeds to identify individuals entering buildings by matching their faces against a database of enrolled students and staff. Facial biometric data will be collected during student registration without separate opt-in consent. The system will flag unrecognised individuals to campus security for manual review. No Data Protection Impact Assessment has been conducted. The facial recognition model will be sourced from a commercial provider and has not been independently audited for demographic bias. Historical studies have shown that such systems exhibit significantly higher error rates for women and people of colour. The research team plans to retain facial embeddings for the duration of each academic year.

retrieved_requirements.text: Research must contribute to society and human well-being — discuss how applications may harm individuals, groups or society and ways to mitigate such harms
Avoid harm — consider how data, code or other outcomes of research may cause harm
Manage personnel and resources to enhance quality of working life — consider how participation of annotators/crowdworkers may cause benefits or harms
Discuss dual use potential — ways research could be misused and mitigations
All human research subjects or participants must receive appropriate compensation — respect minimum hourly rate in region where work is carried out
Respect terms of datasets with defined licences (CC 4.0, MIT etc)
Assess and communicate degree to which datasets are representative of intended population — claims of diverse representation must be substantiated
Consult local laws before collecting or analysing bulk surveillance data — surveillance must not predict protected categories or endanger individual well-being
Communicate whether approach could facilitate deceptive interactions causing harm (theft, fraud, harassment), impersonate public figures, influence political processes, or promote hate speech
High-risk models must be released with safeguards for controlled use (e.g. code of conduct for access)
Have you considered whether your NLP/ML system could out, profile or endanger vulnerable individuals?
Assess whether organisation is obligated to conduct a FRIA (deployer of high-risk AI system)
State the intended purpose of the AI system
Describe the intended use — specific deployment scenarios distinct from intended purpose
Reference instructions for use provided by the AI system provider
Determine FRIA outcome status: Unacceptable Risk / High Residual Risk / Risks Acceptable / Risks Mitigated
AI system must not deprive end-users or affected persons of ability to make decisions about their own lives or remove basic freedoms
AI system must not autonomously make decisions about fundamental personal, economic, social or political issues normally decided by humans, without human oversight and redress
AI system must not subordinate, coerce, deceive, manipulate, objectify or dehumanize end-users or affected persons
AI system must not purposely stimulate attachment or addiction
AI system should give operators and end-users ability to control, direct and intervene in basic operations
End-users and affected persons must receive comprehensible information about the AI logic, significance and envisaged consequences
Possible negative social impacts on certain groups must be mitigated, including impacts beyond algorithmic bias
Methods to identify and mitigate negative social impacts in medium and longer term must be documented in proposal
AI system must not unduly or unfairly reduce psychological and emotional well-being of end-users and stakeholders
AI systems in media, communications, politics, social analytics must be assessed for potential to negatively impact democratic processes and social relations
It must be made clear to end-users they are interacting with an AI system
Purpose, capabilities, limitations, benefits and risks of AI system must be openly communicated to end-users and stakeholders
AI system must allow for human oversight and control over decision cycles and operation, unless compelling justification provided
Use formal methodologies for explainability where possible (XAI, Transparency by Design, Model Cards)
Test whether users understand they are interacting with non-human agent
Ensure AI stakeholders, users and subjects have ethical complaint communication channels
Have you considered whether your AI system could be misused or diverted from its original purpose (function creep)?
Assess whether research could have detrimental legal, economic or social consequences for participants
Declare intentions of study: deception, illegal activity, intrusive/harmful topics, military role, dual purpose
Describe general characteristics of target population (vulnerability status, children, staff/students)
Will research use a gatekeeper? If yes: role, position, dependent relationship?
Identify risks to researcher: intrusive topic, emotional, physical, other, none
For each researcher risk: estimate impact, likelihood, detail mitigation
Declare if dependent relationship exists between researcher and participants
Identify risks to participants: inconvenience, physical, emotional, reputational, financial, privacy, intrusive, harmful
Outline direct benefits of participation
Participant Information Leaflet appropriate for target audience (plain English, accessible format)
Have you considered whether research could be misdirected for dual purpose / harm?
Have you considered whether your recruitment could unintentionally include vulnerable participants?
Have you considered the worst-case scenario for each identified risk?
Have you considered the impact on personal autonomy of the technology being researched?
AI system should be designed for universal accessibility — usable by end-users with different abilities
Ensure interface design honours universal accessibility principles
List inclusion/exclusion criteria for participant selection
Have you considered anti-discrimination and bias implications of your AI/technology research?
Consider and ameliorate negative impacts of approach on political, social or natural environment
Consider whether technology can be used to discriminate, exclude or negatively impact people including healthcare, education or credit access
Inspect suspected biases or limitations in models/datasets — ascertain whether they encode bias against gender, race, sexuality or other protected characteristics
Specify what humans can and cannot do — ability to correct outputs, ability to opt out, override capability
For granular impacts (e.g. discrimination based on sex, race, gender under Art 21), specify the category of impact
Assess whether notification to market surveillance authority is required or exempt under Art 46-1
Research proposal must specify steps to ensure data about people is representative of target population and reflects diversity or is sufficiently neutral
Document how bias in input data and algorithmic design will be identified and avoided
Input, training and output data must all be analysed for bias — verify data accounts for diversity in gender, race, age, sexual orientation, national origin, religion, health, disability
Analyse training data for relevance and representativeness — undertake formal bias assessment
Check for algorithmic bias during detailed development phase using methods such as counterfactual evaluation
Do participants require support to give consent?
Is assent required from participants due to vulnerability?
Is Garda vetting required?
Could participants reveal legally disclosable information (child protection, malpractice)?
Consider and ameliorate biases in data and biased code/model outputs
Respect intellectual property and data/code ownership — cite creators of artefacts used and use them as intended (respect licences)
Respect privacy — obtain informed consent where required and protect personally identifiable information
Give comprehensive evaluation of systems and their impacts including analysis of possible risks of applications
Specify whether project involves humans/their data OR animals
Specify what project involves: human participants/data only, biological samples (various levels)
Will consent be obtained from participants (or proxy/guardian)?
Does the project require a Consent Declaration under HRR?
Does the project include an intervention?
Describe time commitment of participants
How will consent be obtained and by whom?
Time interval between giving information and securing consent (min 7 days recommended)
How can participants withdraw consent and/or their data?
Datasets should minimise exposure of personally identifiable information unless informed consent is provided
Datasets with real data of real people must have explicit consent of participants, or explain why consent was not obtained
Authors must confirm datasets are still available for use — deprecated datasets should not be used except for audit or critical assessment
Consider whether technology can be used to harm, injure or kill people through direct application, side effects or misuse
Provide data and model documentation via structured templates (Model Cards, Datasheets for Datasets)
Provide licences for released data or models including intended use and limitations
Make accessible all information required to understand research artefacts (code, environment, weights, hyperparameters) to enable external scrutiny
Disclose information sufficient for reproduction of results
Record necessity status: FRIA Required or FRIA Not Required
Record when FRIA was created, modified, submitted, accepted
Record who conducted the FRIA (organisation, personnel, contributors)
Record identifier/version of FRIA — must be regularly updated
Record conformance to any codes of conduct
Describe deployer's processes in which the AI system will be used
Specify the period of time and frequency the AI system is intended to be used
Assess the likelihood that those persons/groups will be affected
Describe human oversight measures in place
Specify measures to be taken if risks materialise — internal governance arrangements and complaint mechanisms
If a DPIA already exists under GDPR, document how it complements the FRIA — what it covers and what gaps remain
Complete the FRIA questionnaire template provided by the AI Office
Document whether an automated tool was used to support the FRIA process
Have you considered whether the same AI system with one intended purpose could have different intended uses causing different rights impacts?
Have you considered whether pseudonymised or anonymised data could still lead to identification of affected individuals?
Have you assessed impact at the granular level — not just non-discrimination but which specific grounds (sex, race, disability, age)?
Data must be acquired, stored and used in a manner auditable by humans
Traceability measures must be considered for the AI system's entire lifecycle
Research proposal should explain how decisions made by system will be explainable to users, including reasons for decisions
Development processes must keep records of all relevant decisions regarding ethical compliance
Document how ethically and socially undesirable effects will be detected, stopped and prevented from reoccurring
Research proposal should include evaluation of possible ethics risks including risk assessment procedures and post-deployment mitigation
All AI systems should be auditable by independent third parties — system should generate human-accessible logs
Ensure ability to go back to each state the system has been in to determine training data used at any point
Establish traceability and accountability measures covering: design methods, testing methods, system outcomes, and alternative decision scenarios
Code must be documented and explained within software and ancillary documentation
Ensure audit controls check performance and record decisions about system purpose and functioning
Communicate system capabilities and limitations to stakeholders in clear, understandable and proactive manner
Establish processes to obtain user feedback and mechanisms to adapt system in response
Establish and implement plans supporting operational compliance with ethical requirements
Update data, access, security and risk management policies to account for ethical requirements
Have you assessed the environmental footprint of your AI system's compute and supply chain?
Applicant must be identified with name, staff/student number, email, school/department
Applicant must confirm role on project (PI / Non-PI / Other)
Applicant must have read and understood Trinity Policy on Good Research Practice
Applicant must have completed Research Integrity Training Module
All Trinity collaborators must be listed with role, name, email, school
All non-Trinity collaborators must be listed with role, name, affiliation, country
Project title, data collection start/end dates, project end date must be specified
State research aims, objectives, research question or hypothesis (100 words)
Lay summary including background, rationale, research approach, study design (250 words)
Declare whether project is funded
Declare if study is phased
For each site: licence, permission, or ethics approval status
Outline data collection methods
Approximate target population size and proposed sample size
Justification for sample size
Outline method of analysis (100 words)
Outline sampling method
Step-by-step description of participant recruitment process
Declare any conflict of interest from PI or collaborators
RPAMS number if applicable
List all sources of funding with type (industrial, state, philanthropic, other)
Funder-specific requirements or obligations for ethics committee attention
Will results be used/disclosed for commercial purposes?
Conflict of interest from funding or commercialisation
All required attachments uploaded (consent forms, certificates, PIL, recruitment material, methods instruments, DPO letter)
Applicant declaration: accuracy, compliance with Good Research Practice, GDPR, HRR, TCD policies
PI declaration and approval (if applicant ≠ PI)
Supervisor declaration and approval (all student applications)
Be fair and take action not to discriminate — consider and ameliorate biases the approach may perpetuate
Consider possible risks and impacts of the research approach itself (not just applications)
Design and implement systems that are robustly and usably secure — especially systems close to production deployment
Research involving human participants must follow existing institutional protocols (IRB or equivalent) — if no formal process exists, undergo equivalent informal ethics review
Consider risk that applications could open security vulnerabilities or cause serious accidents — recommend or implement protections

Research must not build upon or facilitate illegal activity — strongly discourage work that denies rights to privacy, speech, health, liberty, security, legal personhood, freedom of conscience/religion
Use privacy protocols, encryption and anonymisation to reduce risk of data leakage — stronger measures for sensitive data
Identify categories of natural persons and groups likely to be affected by the AI system in the specific context
Identify consequences and impacts on affected persons/groups
If required: notify market surveillance authority of FRIA results
AI system must process personal data lawfully, fairly and transparently
Data minimisation and data protection by design and by default must be integrated
Appropriate technical and organisational measures must be in place to safeguard rights of data subjects (anonymisation, pseudonymisation, encryption, aggregation)
Ensure processing of personal data complies with GDPR — prepare specific data protection policy
Ensure no new personal data collected or created during development or use unless necessary
Are you processing any personal data for your research project?
If processing recruitment data: outline storage, confidentiality, destruction plan
How will participants be informed about use of their personal data?
Specify data collection setting(s)
List all types of personal data processed (including special category)
Does project involve special category data or criminal conviction data?
Describe security measures for data access, storage, transfer
Have you considered indirect identifiability from participant profiles in small populations?
Have you considered whether anonymised data could be re-identified?
Access computing and communication resources only when authorised or compelled by public good
Identify specific risks of harm to fundamental rights
Strong security measures must prevent data breaches and leakages
If creating inferred personal data or aggregating datasets, new data must receive same protection level as original data
Applicant must have completed Data Protection Training within last 24 months
Identify all countries where data is collected or processed
Specify data sources: primary only, secondary only, or both
Is the project Health Research under HRR 2018?
Are you processing any pseudonymised (coded) data?
Are you processing personal data for participant recruitment?
Name each data collection site/source
Was a DPIA required at each site/source?
Specify if methods are exclusively low-risk (quality assurance, anonymous surveys, public observation, audits, public domain data)
All Trinity researchers completed GDPR training?
All Trinity staff/students familiar with Personal Data Breach Procedural Guidelines?
What is Trinity's role: Controller, Joint Controller, or Processor?
Number of participants whose personal data is processed (≥100 or <100)
Is personal data shared within Trinity (outside research team)?
Is personal data shared with third parties outside Trinity?
Are personal data transferred outside UK/EEA?
Detail how long personal data retained in identifiable/coded format
For each processing risk: list risk, severity/impact, likelihood, mitigation actions
